content.decision_question: Should reform prioritize the selection/admissions mechanism itself, or should it sequence quality investment in comprehensive schools first as a precondition for reducing demand for early selection?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence (e.g., experimental or quasi-experimental studies isolating quality-investment effects on selective-track application rates) could clarify whether quality investment causally reduces demand, but cannot resolve whether status-signaling motives for track-seeking are separable from quality concerns, since this involves family preference structures not fully observable through current instruments.
content.tension: Reforming admissions procedures or delaying selection ages addresses the supply-side sorting mechanism directly, while investing in comprehensive-school quality addresses the demand-side distrust that drives families toward selective tracks; each targets a different point in the causal chain and evidence does not establish which lever is more effective or whether both are needed.
content.title: Targeting the selection mechanism versus targeting selection demand
value_poles: addressing root mechanisms versus addressing root causes
efficient use of limited reform capacity
